TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods

clusters.label: Approximate Nearest Neighbor
members: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Estimation

concepts.name: retrieval-augmented generation
concepts.description: A neural generation approach that combines a pretrained parametric model with retrieval from an external non-parametric memory. During generation, the model conditions on retrieved documents or passages so it can produce more factual, knowledge-grounded outputs. Its behavior can be influenced by changing the retrieval source without modifying only the model parameters.
concepts.match_reason: Candidate [0] is a specific pre-training method using retrieval, not the general retrieval-augmented generation concept.

concepts.name: large language model
concepts.description: 大语言模型是指参数规模显著增大的预训练语言模型，通常通过在大规模文本语料上训练获得。随着参数与训练规模的扩展，这类模型往往具备更强的自然语言理解与生成能力，并可能在提示适当时表现出更复杂的能力（如一定程度的涌现或推理）。
concepts.match_reason: A large language model is a specific type of neural language model, not the same as the broader pre-trained neural language model or language model concepts.

concepts.name: open-domain question answering
concepts.description: Open-domain question answering is a question answering setting where the model answers questions using information from a large, unstructured corpus rather than a provided passage or a closed, narrow domain. It typically requires retrieving relevant evidence from broad sources and then generating or selecting the correct answer, often relying on general-world knowledge when necessary.

concepts.name: Transformer
concepts.description: A Transformer is a neural network architecture for sequence modeling built around attention-based layers. It removes the need for recurrence and convolution, enabling highly parallel computation and strong performance on tasks such as machine translation and language modeling. Its modular layer structure also supports inserting adaptation modules into the network for specialized behavior.

concepts.name: GPT-3
concepts.description: An autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters designed to generate and predict text from context. It is notable for strong task-agnostic few-shot performance when prompted with examples in text.

concepts.name: question-answering
concepts.description: A language task in which a system must produce an answer to a natural-language question, often by locating relevant information in a passage or knowledge source and synthesizing it into a concise response. It is used to evaluate the model’s question understanding, information retrieval, and comprehension-based reasoning.

concepts.name: Graph view
concepts.description: A graph view is a representation of graph-structured information organized as nodes and edges, where nodes denote entities and edges denote relationships. It explicitly encodes how entities are connected, supporting retrieval and reasoning that rely on relational context, including hierarchical or interconnected structures when present.
concepts.match_reason: Graph view emphasizes structure and connectivity, while text view presents the information as plain text; they are different representations, not the same concept.

concepts.name: dense representation
concepts.description: A dense (continuous) learned vector representation that encodes text, words, entities, or other discrete items in a continuous vector space. The embedding is trained so that semantically or conceptually related items are close under a chosen similarity measure, enabling similarity-based retrieval and robust matching beyond exact term overlap.

concepts.name: BERT
concepts.description: BERT is a language representation model based on deep bidirectional Transformers. It is pre-trained on unlabeled text by conditioning on both left and right context in all layers, and then fine-tuned with a small task-specific output layer for downstream language understanding tasks.

concepts.name: RoBERTa
concepts.description: RoBERTa is a robustly optimized BERT pretraining approach that revisits the original BERT training recipe and systematically studies the effects of key training choices. It is designed to improve downstream language model performance by optimizing pretraining setup and data usage.
concepts.match_reason: RoBERTa is a modified pretraining approach/variant of BERT, not the same concept as BERT itself.

concepts.name: training data size
concepts.description: Training data size is the amount of data available for fitting a model during pretraining or training. It affects how low the model’s loss can reach and how prone it is to overfitting, and it interacts with model size and compute in scaling relationships.

concepts.name: pre-trained neural language model
concepts.description: 预训练神经语言模型是指先在大规模语料上进行预训练学习，再将其用于下游任务或进一步适配的语言模型。它通过学习通用的语言表示来建模词序列的概率分布，从而在多种自然语言处理任务上提升性能。

concepts.name: domain adaptation
concepts.description: The process of adjusting a model so that it works better on a target domain whose data distribution differs from the original training domain. It can be achieved by changing the support data or retrieval source rather than retraining the whole model.

concepts.name: model size
concepts.description: 指机器学习模型的规模，通常用参数数量、层数或整体容量等指标来衡量。增大模型规模往往能提升模型的表达能力与性能，但同时会显著增加训练所需的计算量与存储成本，并影响样本效率与训练损失等表现。

concepts.name: implicit knowledge storage
concepts.description: The storage of factual information in the internal parameters (weights) of a neural network rather than in an explicit external memory. The knowledge is distributed across the model and is accessed indirectly through the network’s computations, enabling generation or prediction from learned internal representations rather than providing directly readable facts.

concepts.name: passage retrieval
concepts.description: The task of selecting candidate passages from a large collection that are likely to contain the answer to a question. It serves as an initial filtering step in open-domain question answering and must balance effectiveness with efficiency.

concepts.name: dense retriever
concepts.description: A dense retriever is a retrieval model that maps queries and documents (or passages) into a shared vector embedding space, then retrieves items by measuring similarity between their embeddings. Instead of relying on sparse lexical overlap, it ranks candidates based on vector-space similarity, often using a neural encoder. It is commonly used as a first-stage component to fetch relevant passages from a large collection for a downstream generator or answering system.
concepts.match_reason: A dense retriever is a retrieval system/class, while the dual-encoder framework is a specific architecture used to build such systems, so they are not exactly the same concept.

concepts.name: few-shot learning
concepts.description: A learning setting in which a model must perform a new task from only a few examples or simple instructions. It aims to approximate human-like adaptation without requiring large task-specific training sets.

concepts.name: fine-tuning
concepts.description: A training process that adapts a pre-trained model to a particular task using task-specific labeled examples. It specializes the model beyond its general pre-training.

concepts.name: Natural Questions
concepts.description: Natural Questions is a benchmark for open-domain question answering built from real Google search queries and associated answers. It is used to evaluate whether systems can retrieve and use relevant evidence from large text collections.

concepts.name: GPT-2
concepts.description: A transformer-based language model used as a benchmark for evaluating adaptation methods. It is commonly used to compare fine-tuning approaches on language generation tasks.
concepts.match_reason: GPT-2 and GPT-3 are different language model versions; the candidate is not the same concept.

concepts.name: truthfulness
concepts.description: The degree to which generated answers are factually correct rather than misleading or false. In language models, it concerns whether responses avoid repeating incorrect information learned from data or imitation.
concepts.match_reason: Truthfulness is the general property of factual correctness in answers, while TruthfulQA is a specific benchmark used to measure it.

concepts.name: knowledge-intensive task
concepts.description: A task whose successful solution depends heavily on access to factual or world knowledge rather than only pattern recognition or local reasoning. Question answering and fact checking are common examples.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is broader, while the candidate is specifically a knowledge-intensive NLP task; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: GraphRAG
concepts.description: GraphRAG is a retrieval-augmented generation approach for graph-structured documents. It builds a graph index from source content, retrieves relevant subgraphs or neighborhoods, and combines their textual information with the graph’s topological context to support more informed generation. It typically also produces community-level summaries of related entities to answer both local (specific) and global (broader) questions over large corpora.

concepts.name: Multi-hop reasoning
concepts.description: A reasoning process that reaches an answer by chaining together multiple intermediate facts or relations. It is commonly used when information is distributed across several connected pieces of evidence.

concepts.name: retrieval process
concepts.description: The procedure for finding, selecting, and returning information relevant to a query from a stored collection or external corpus. In retrieval-augmented systems, it provides evidence to the generator to ground its responses and improve factuality.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the retrieval procedure itself, while the candidates are retrieval-augmented generation/model approaches that use retrieval as a component.

concepts.name: latent knowledge retriever
concepts.description: A latent knowledge retriever is a module that selects relevant documents or passages from a large text corpus in response to an input query or context. It provides external evidence or background knowledge to downstream language models so they can consult information beyond what is stored in their parameters.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a retriever module, while the candidate is a specific pre-training approach that uses retrieval; related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: false beliefs
concepts.description: Widely held but incorrect ideas about a topic that can lead people to reason or answer questions wrongly. In evaluation settings, such false beliefs may be used to construct questions that test whether a model reproduces human-like mistakes, and in language models they can be reinforced by training text, resulting in plausible but false answers.

concepts.name: recurrent neural network
concepts.description: A neural network architecture that processes sequences one step at a time while carrying forward a hidden state that summarizes previous inputs. This recurrence makes it suitable for ordered data, but it limits parallelization because later steps depend on earlier ones.

concepts.name: convolutional neural network
concepts.description: A neural network architecture that uses learned convolutional filters to detect local patterns in structured inputs such as images or sequences. It shares parameters across positions and can exploit local regularities efficiently.

concepts.name: encoder-decoder architecture
concepts.description: A neural network design in which one component encodes an input sequence into an internal representation and a second component decodes that representation into an output sequence. It is widely used for sequence-to-sequence tasks such as translation.

concepts.name: attention mechanism
concepts.description: A method that lets a model weight different parts of an input or intermediate representation according to their relevance for a current prediction. It improves sequence modeling by providing direct, content-based access to information without relying only on a fixed-size summary.

concepts.name: BLEU
concepts.description: An automatic evaluation metric for machine translation that measures the overlap between a candidate translation and one or more reference translations. Higher scores generally indicate closer agreement with the references.

concepts.name: WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task
concepts.description: A standard machine translation benchmark from the 2014 Workshop on Machine Translation for translating English text into German. It is used to compare the quality and efficiency of translation systems under a common test setting.

concepts.name: WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task
concepts.description: A standard machine translation benchmark from the 2014 Workshop on Machine Translation for translating English text into French. It is used to compare translation systems and report state-of-the-art performance under a shared evaluation setup.
concepts.match_reason: The candidate is the English-to-German WMT 2014 task, while the incoming concept is English-to-French; they are different translation benchmarks.

concepts.name: English constituency parsing
concepts.description: A syntactic parsing task that analyzes an English sentence into a hierarchy of nested constituents such as noun phrases and verb phrases. The output is a phrase-structure tree showing how words group into larger grammatical units.

concepts.name: BERT pretraining
concepts.description: BERT pretraining is the process of training a BERT-style language model on large text corpora before task-specific fine-tuning. It is intended to learn general language representations that transfer well to many downstream tasks.
concepts.match_reason: BERT pretraining is a training process, while BERT is the model itself and RoBERTa is a specific variant of the pretraining recipe, not the same concept.

concepts.name: hyperparameter choice
concepts.description: A hyperparameter choice is a selection of training settings specified before optimization, such as learning rate, batch size, or schedule. These choices can strongly affect model convergence and final performance, even when the model architecture and data are fixed.

concepts.name: GLUE
concepts.description: GLUE is a benchmark suite for evaluating general language understanding systems across multiple natural language processing tasks. It is used to compare models on standardized tests of linguistic and reasoning abilities.

concepts.name: RACE
concepts.description: RACE is a reading comprehension benchmark constructed from English exams, designed to test a model's ability to answer questions based on passages. It emphasizes understanding of longer contexts and inference over text.

concepts.name: SQuAD
concepts.description: SQuAD is a question answering benchmark built from Wikipedia articles and human-generated questions. It evaluates a model's ability to extract or identify answer spans from a context passage.

concepts.name: kNN-LM
concepts.description: A language model that augments a pre-trained neural language model by linearly interpolating its predictions with a k-nearest neighbors model. It retrieves similar contexts from a datastore in embedding space to improve next-word prediction without additional training.

concepts.name: k-nearest neighbors model
concepts.description: A predictive model that makes decisions by retrieving the k closest stored examples to a query and combining their information. In language modeling, it can provide next-token evidence from similar contexts instead of relying only on parametric weights.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the general k-nearest neighbors model, while the candidate is a specific kNN-augmented language model variant (kNN-LM), not the same concept.

concepts.name: embedding space
concepts.description: A vector space in which linguistic items are represented as continuous embeddings so that geometric distance reflects similarity. Nearby points in the space correspond to contexts or sequences that the model considers alike.

concepts.name: nearest neighbor datastore
concepts.description: A stored collection of examples that can be searched for the nearest neighbors of a query representation. In language modeling, it supplies retrieved contexts and targets that can be combined with model predictions at inference time.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the datastore itself, while both candidates describe models that use nearest neighbors/datastores rather than the stored collection.

concepts.name: nearest neighbor search
concepts.description: A retrieval procedure that finds the stored items closest to a query according to a distance measure. It is used to identify similar contexts or examples that can inform a model's prediction.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the retrieval procedure itself, while the candidates are a datastore and a k-NN model, which are related but not the same thing.

concepts.name: rare pattern prediction
concepts.description: The ability to predict infrequent sequences or structures that occur only sparsely in training data. In language modeling, this often includes unusual word combinations and low-frequency factual associations.

concepts.name: empirical scaling laws
concepts.description: Regular quantitative relationships that describe how a model’s performance changes as key resources such as model size, dataset size, or compute scale up. In language modeling, they are used to predict performance trends across many orders of magnitude and to guide resource allocation.

concepts.name: cross-entropy loss
concepts.description: A loss function that measures the difference between predicted probability distributions and the true target distribution. In language modeling, it is a standard measure of performance and uncertainty, with lower values indicating better predictive accuracy.

concepts.name: power law
concepts.description: A functional relationship in which one quantity varies as a constant times another quantity raised to an exponent. In scaling analyses, power laws capture smooth, predictable changes over wide ranges of model size, data size, or compute.

concepts.name: compute
concepts.description: The total computational resources consumed during training, typically measured in operations or similar cost units. It constrains feasible model and data choices and determines the tradeoff between training scale and efficiency.

concepts.name: network width
concepts.description: The number of units or channels in a neural network layer. It is an architectural parameter that can affect capacity, but within a wide range it has only a minimal effect compared with the dominant scaling variables.

concepts.name: network depth
concepts.description: The number of layers in a neural network. It is an architectural parameter that can affect representational capacity, but within a wide range it has only a minimal effect compared with the dominant scaling variables.
concepts.match_reason: Network depth means the number of layers, while network width means the number of units or channels per layer; they are different architectural parameters.

concepts.name: overfitting
concepts.description: The tendency of a model to fit idiosyncrasies of the training set that do not generalize well. It depends on the balance between model size and dataset size and can be estimated with simple scaling equations.

concepts.name: training speed
concepts.description: The rate at which a model’s loss improves during optimization. It depends on model size and can be characterized by simple equations in scaling analyses.

concepts.name: compute budget
concepts.description: A fixed allowance of computational resources available for training. It imposes a constraint that must be divided among model size, data size, and training duration to achieve the best result.
concepts.match_reason: “Compute budget” is the allowed resource limit, while “compute” here refers to the total resources actually consumed; related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: sample efficiency
concepts.description: The ability of a model to achieve strong performance using relatively little training data. Higher sample efficiency means that a given amount of data produces more improvement in loss or accuracy.

concepts.name: compute-efficient training
concepts.description: A training strategy that seeks the best possible performance for a fixed amount of compute. It typically involves choosing model size, dataset size, and training duration to maximize return on computational investment.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a training strategy to maximize performance under fixed compute, while the candidates are the compute resource itself or its allowance, not the strategy.

concepts.name: convergence
concepts.description: The state in which training has largely exhausted further improvement under the optimization process. Stopping before convergence can be preferable when compute is limited and additional training yields diminishing returns.

concepts.name: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training
concepts.description: A pre-training approach for language models that augments parametric memory with document retrieval. It trains the model to fetch relevant text from a large corpus so knowledge can be accessed more modularly and interpreted more directly.
concepts.match_reason: The candidate is a generic pre-trained language model, while the incoming concept is a specific retrieval-augmented pre-training approach that adds document retrieval during training.

concepts.name: masked language modeling
concepts.description: A self-supervised learning objective in which some tokens in an input sequence are hidden and the model is trained to predict them. It is widely used to learn language representations from unlabeled text.

concepts.name: backpropagation through retrieval
concepts.description: A training approach that propagates gradients through a retrieval operation so the retrieval component can be optimized end to end. It is used when the model must learn which documents to retrieve as part of the prediction process.

concepts.name: explicit knowledge storage
concepts.description: The storage of factual information in an external, inspectable memory such as retrieved documents or a structured knowledge source. This form of storage makes the source of information easier to access and interpret.
concepts.match_reason: This is the opposite concept: explicit storage keeps factual information in external, inspectable memory, while implicit storage keeps it in internal model parameters.

concepts.name: sparse vector space model
concepts.description: A retrieval model that represents text as a sparse vector over terms, assigning nonzero weight only to a small subset of vocabulary items. Such models are effective for lexical matching and are widely used in traditional information retrieval systems.

concepts.name: TF-IDF
concepts.description: A term-weighting scheme that scores terms by combining their frequency in a document with their inverse frequency across a corpus. It is used to emphasize informative words while downweighting common terms in retrieval and text analysis.

concepts.name: BM25
concepts.description: A ranking function used in information retrieval that estimates how well a document matches a query based on term overlap and term saturation effects. It is a standard sparse retrieval method and is often a strong baseline for passage ranking.

concepts.name: dual-encoder framework
concepts.description: A neural retrieval architecture that encodes a query and a candidate passage separately into vector representations. Relevance is computed by comparing the two vectors, which makes large-scale retrieval efficient because passage representations can be precomputed.

concepts.name: Lucene-BM25 system
concepts.description: A retrieval system built with the Lucene search engine and BM25 ranking. It is commonly used as a strong sparse-retrieval baseline for passage ranking and document search.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a Lucene-based retrieval system using BM25, while the candidate is only the BM25 ranking function itself, so they are not the same concept.

concepts.name: top-20 passage retrieval accuracy
concepts.description: An evaluation metric measuring whether at least one correct passage appears among the top 20 retrieved passages. It reflects how well a retrieval system surfaces relevant evidence early in the ranked list.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific evaluation metric (top-20 retrieval accuracy), while the candidate is the retrieval task itself, not the same concept.

concepts.name: end-to-end QA system
concepts.description: A question answering system that performs the full pipeline from retrieving evidence to producing an answer. Its performance depends on both retrieval quality and answer prediction quality.

concepts.name: open-domain QA benchmark
concepts.description: A standardized evaluation dataset or suite used to compare open-domain question answering systems. It provides common tasks and metrics for measuring retrieval and answer quality across models.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is an evaluation benchmark/dataset, while the candidate is the open-domain QA task itself, so they denote different things.

concepts.name: non-parametric memory
concepts.description: An external memory store that is accessed directly rather than encoded into model weights. It typically contains explicit items such as documents or vectors and can be updated independently of the generator.
concepts.match_reason: Non-parametric memory is explicit external storage accessed directly, while implicit knowledge storage is knowledge embedded in model weights and accessed indirectly; they are contrasting concepts.

concepts.name: pre-trained seq2seq model
concepts.description: A sequence-to-sequence neural model pretrained on large text corpora and then adapted to downstream tasks. It maps an input sequence to an output sequence, making it suitable for tasks such as generation and question answering.
concepts.match_reason: A pre-trained seq2seq model is an encoder-decoder sequence-to-sequence architecture, while a pre-trained neural language model is a model for assigning probabilities to text sequences; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: dense vector index
concepts.description: An index that represents items as dense embedding vectors to support similarity-based retrieval. It allows fast nearest-neighbor access to passages or documents using continuous representations rather than sparse lexical matching.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a vector index/data structure for ANN retrieval, while candidate 0 is the embedding representation itself and candidate 1 is a retrieval system using such vectors, not the index.

concepts.name: retrieve-and-extract architecture
concepts.description: A knowledge-intensive NLP architecture that first retrieves relevant passages and then extracts the answer from them. It is designed to ground predictions in external text instead of generating answers only from model parameters.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a full retrieve-then-extract QA architecture, while the candidate is only a retrieval module for fetching evidence, not the extraction architecture itself.

concepts.name: knowledge-intensive NLP task
concepts.description: A natural language processing task that depends heavily on factual world knowledge and precise information access. Such tasks often expose the limits of purely parametric models and benefit from retrieval over external sources.

concepts.name: provenance
concepts.description: Information about the source or origin of a model's output or decision. In language systems, provenance helps show where a generated fact or answer came from and supports transparency and verification.

concepts.name: language model
concepts.description: A probabilistic model that assigns likelihoods to sequences of text and can generate or predict words based on preceding context. Language models are used to model and produce natural language.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the general notion of a language model, while candidate [0] is a specific pre-trained neural variant and [1] is a training objective, not the same concept.

concepts.name: pre-training
concepts.description: A training stage in which a model is first fit on a large corpus of general data before being adapted to specific tasks. It is used to learn broad linguistic patterns and representations.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is general model pre-training, while the candidate is the specific BERT pretraining procedure, so they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: autoregressive language model
concepts.description: A language model that generates text token by token by conditioning each next token on the preceding tokens. This formulation is commonly used for text generation and conditional prediction.
concepts.match_reason: An autoregressive language model is a specific type of language model, not the same concept as the broader category.

concepts.name: translation
concepts.description: A language task that maps text from one language into another while preserving meaning. It is a standard benchmark for evaluating natural language processing systems.

concepts.name: cloze task
concepts.description: A task in which a system must fill in a missing word or span in a sentence or passage. It evaluates how well the model uses context to infer the omitted content.

concepts.name: unscrambling words
concepts.description: A language task that requires restoring a scrambled sequence of letters or words to its intended form. It tests pattern recognition and on-the-fly reasoning over character or token order.

concepts.name: 3-digit arithmetic
concepts.description: A reasoning task involving arithmetic operations on numbers with three digits. It is used to test whether a model can carry out exact symbolic computation from context.

concepts.name: news article generation
concepts.description: The generation of news-style articles in natural language. It is used to evaluate whether a model can produce coherent, realistic text in a specific genre.

concepts.name: web corpus
concepts.description: A large collection of text gathered from the web and used as training data for language models. Web corpora are broad and diverse but may contain noisy or duplicated content.

concepts.name: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Estimation
concepts.description: A training mechanism for dense retrieval models that selects negative examples from an approximate nearest neighbor index built over the corpus. By continually refreshing the index during learning, it surfaces harder and more realistic negatives that better match the distribution of irrelevant documents seen at test time.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific training method for dense retrieval using refreshed ANN negatives, not the generic nearest-neighbor search procedure or datastore.

concepts.name: Approximate Nearest Neighbor
concepts.description: A method for finding items in a large space that are close to a query item without computing exact distances to every candidate. It is commonly used to speed up similarity search by trading a small amount of precision for much higher efficiency.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the general idea of approximate nearest-neighbor search, while the candidates are exact nearest neighbor search, a datastore, a k-NN model, and a specific training method using ANN as a component—not the same concept.

concepts.name: Approximate Nearest Neighbor index
concepts.description: An index structure organized to support approximate nearest neighbor search over a collection of items. It enables fast retrieval of likely close candidates and is often updated as the underlying representations change.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is an ANN index data structure, while the candidates are a search method, a datastore, or a training technique that uses such an index; none denote the same concept.

concepts.name: BERT-Siamese model
concepts.description: A Siamese neural retrieval architecture that uses BERT to encode a query and a document into comparable vector representations. Relevance is then estimated from the similarity between the two embeddings, typically with a simple scoring function such as a dot product.

concepts.name: dot product
concepts.description: A vector similarity measure computed by multiplying corresponding components and summing the results. In learned retrieval systems, it is often used as a scoring function for comparing query and document representations.

concepts.name: evidence aggregation
concepts.description: Evidence aggregation is the process of combining information from multiple supporting sources to form a more complete or reliable answer or prediction. It is important when no single source contains all necessary information.

concepts.name: TriviaQA
concepts.description: TriviaQA is a question answering benchmark composed of trivia questions paired with evidence from documents such as web pages and Wikipedia. It is used to test a system's ability to answer questions using retrieved textual evidence.

concepts.name: Low-Rank Adaptation
concepts.description: A parameter-efficient method for adapting a pre-trained neural network by freezing the original weights and adding trainable low-rank matrices to selected layers. It reduces the number of trainable parameters and memory use while preserving model quality on downstream tasks.

concepts.name: full fine-tuning
concepts.description: A model adaptation approach that retrains all of a pre-trained model’s parameters on a new task or domain. It can achieve strong performance but becomes increasingly expensive as model size grows.
concepts.match_reason: Full fine-tuning is a specific type of fine-tuning that updates all parameters, while the candidate ‘fine-tuning’ is the broader concept, not the same one.

concepts.name: pre-trained model weights
concepts.description: The learned parameters of a model obtained during large-scale pre-training on general data. These weights provide the starting point for later adaptation to specific tasks or domains and may be kept fixed during parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the learned weights/parameters from pre-training, while the candidate is the pre-training stage itself; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: rank decomposition matrices
concepts.description: Low-rank trainable matrices used to represent an update to a larger weight matrix through factorization. They enable efficient adaptation by adding a small number of parameters instead of retraining the full matrix.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept refers to the low-rank matrices themselves, while the candidate is the broader adaptation method that uses them.

concepts.name: adapters
concepts.description: Small trainable modules inserted into a neural network to adapt a pre-trained model to new tasks while keeping most original parameters frozen. They improve parameter efficiency but can add extra computation at inference time.
concepts.match_reason: Adapters and LoRA are related parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, but they are different techniques: adapters insert new modules, while LoRA adds low-rank weight updates.

concepts.name: inference latency
concepts.description: The delay between providing an input to a model and receiving its output. In model adaptation methods, added modules can increase this latency by introducing extra computation during prediction.

concepts.name: rank-deficiency
concepts.description: A property of matrix updates or transformations in which the effective dimensionality is lower than the full dimensionality. In language model adaptation, it can explain why low-rank parameterizations are effective.
concepts.match_reason: Rank-deficiency is a property of reduced effective dimensionality, while the candidate is a specific low-rank matrix parameterization used to implement updates.

concepts.name: language model adaptation
concepts.description: The process of modifying a pre-trained language model so it performs well on a new task or domain. It often aims to reuse existing knowledge while changing only a small set of parameters or components.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the general adaptation of a pre-trained language model, while the candidates are a specific domain-adaptation setting and a full fine-tuning variant, neither of which is the same concept.

concepts.name: PyTorch
concepts.description: A machine learning framework for building and training neural networks. It provides model and tensor abstractions that can be extended with additional adaptation modules such as low-rank updates.

concepts.name: DeBERTa
concepts.description: A transformer-based language model used as a benchmark for comparing adaptation methods. It is designed for strong performance on language understanding tasks and is commonly fine-tuned for downstream applications.
concepts.match_reason: DeBERTa is a different transformer language model; GPT-2 and BERT are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: Adam
concepts.description: A gradient-based optimization algorithm that adapts learning rates using estimates of first and second moments of the gradients. It is widely used for training neural networks and fine-tuning large language models.

concepts.name: TruthfulQA
concepts.description: A benchmark designed to measure whether a language model gives truthful answers to questions. It evaluates responses on questions that are likely to elicit false answers because they align with common human misconceptions or false beliefs.
concepts.match_reason: TruthfulQA is a benchmark for truthful answering to misconception-prone questions, while TriviaQA is a trivia question-answering benchmark with supporting evidence; they are different tasks.

concepts.name: imitation of text
concepts.description: A learning behavior in which a model reproduces patterns, statements, and associations found in its training text. This can cause the model to mirror both correct information and common falsehoods present in the data.

concepts.name: training distribution
concepts.description: The distribution of examples present in the data used to train a model. If false statements are common in that data, a model may learn to reproduce them as likely outputs.

concepts.name: training objectives
concepts.description: The optimization goals used during model training to shape the model's behavior. Different objectives can encourage different kinds of output, such as imitation, factuality, or task-specific accuracy.

concepts.name: scaling up models
concepts.description: Increasing model size, often by adding more parameters or capacity. This can improve many language tasks, but it does not necessarily improve truthfulness when the model has learned false patterns from data.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the act of increasing model size, while the candidate is a set of quantitative laws describing how performance changes with scale; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: GPT-Neo
concepts.description: An open language model family modeled after GPT-style autoregressive architectures. It is used to generate text and can be evaluated for factuality and truthfulness.
concepts.match_reason: GPT-Neo is a different open model family; the candidates are GPT-3 and GPT-2, which are separate models, not the same concept.

concepts.name: T5
concepts.description: A text-to-text transformer model that frames all language tasks as converting one text sequence into another. It can be adapted to many tasks through fine-tuning on labeled examples or other objectives.
concepts.match_reason: T5 is a specific text-to-text Transformer model, while the candidate is the general Transformer architecture.

concepts.name: chain of thought
concepts.description: A chain of thought is a sequence of intermediate reasoning steps used to work through a problem. It helps break complex reasoning into smaller, explicit steps that can support more accurate conclusions.

concepts.name: chain of thought prompting
concepts.description: Chain of thought prompting is a prompting method that includes examples of step-by-step reasoning in the prompt. It encourages a language model to produce intermediate reasoning before giving an answer, which can improve performance on complex reasoning tasks.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a prompting method that elicits step-by-step reasoning, while the candidate is the reasoning sequence itself, not the prompting technique.

concepts.name: few-shot demonstration
concepts.description: A few-shot demonstration is an example included in a prompt to show a model the desired input-output pattern. Using a small number of exemplars can steer the model toward the intended task format or reasoning style.
concepts.match_reason: A few-shot demonstration is an in-prompt exemplar used to guide a model, while few-shot learning is the broader learning setting of adapting from few examples; related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: arithmetic reasoning
concepts.description: Arithmetic reasoning is the ability to solve problems that require numerical computation and manipulation of numbers. It involves applying arithmetic operations and intermediate calculations to reach an answer.
concepts.match_reason: "Arithmetic reasoning" is a broad ability to solve numerical problems, while "3-digit arithmetic" is a specific task variant involving three-digit numbers.

concepts.name: commonsense reasoning
concepts.description: Commonsense reasoning is the ability to draw on everyday knowledge about how the world works. It allows a system to infer plausible conclusions from ordinary situations and implicit assumptions.

concepts.name: symbolic reasoning
concepts.description: Symbolic reasoning is the manipulation of symbols according to formal rules to derive conclusions. It is used in tasks that require structured, logic-like inference rather than direct pattern matching.

concepts.name: GSM8K benchmark
concepts.description: GSM8K is a benchmark of grade-school math word problems used to evaluate mathematical reasoning in language models. It measures how well a system can solve multi-step numerical problems from natural language questions.

concepts.name: math word problem
concepts.description: A math word problem is a natural-language problem that describes a situation requiring mathematical reasoning to solve. It typically combines text understanding with numerical computation and stepwise inference.

concepts.name: fine-tuned GPT-3
concepts.description: Fine-tuned GPT-3 is a version of GPT-3 adapted on additional task-specific data to improve performance on a target task. Fine-tuning adjusts the model beyond its base training to better match a benchmark or application.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a specific fine-tuned GPT-3 model, while the candidates are the fine-tuning process itself or unrelated models/variants.

concepts.name: instruction following
concepts.description: The ability of a language model to produce responses that match a user's requested intent and constraints. It is a practical measure of how well the model behaves as an assistant in response to prompts.

concepts.name: human feedback
concepts.description: Information provided by people about the quality or desirability of model behavior. It can be collected as demonstrations or rankings and used to steer a model toward outputs preferred by humans.

concepts.name: labeler demonstrations
concepts.description: Examples created by human labelers that show the desired behavior of a model for given prompts. They are used as supervised targets when fine-tuning a language model.
aliases: demonstrations of the desired model behavior

concepts.name: supervised learning
concepts.description: A training approach in which a model is optimized to match labeled examples. For language models, it learns to imitate target outputs provided in demonstrations.
concepts.match_reason: Supervised learning is training on labeled targets; masked language modeling is a different self-supervised objective on unlabeled text.

concepts.name: reinforcement learning from human feedback
concepts.description: A training method that uses human preferences over model outputs as feedback to optimize a model's behavior. A reward model or preference signal is learned from rankings, and the model is further updated to produce outputs humans prefer.

concepts.name: InstructGPT
concepts.description: A family of language models fine-tuned to follow user instructions using supervised learning and reinforcement learning from human feedback. The resulting models are optimized to better align outputs with human intent.
concepts.match_reason: InstructGPT is a specific GPT-3-based instruction-following model family trained with supervised learning and RLHF, not just generic fine-tuned GPT-3.

concepts.name: alignment
concepts.description: The degree to which a language model's outputs match human intent, preferences, and values. Aligned models are more useful, truthful, and less likely to produce harmful or irrelevant content.
concepts.match_reason: Alignment is broader than instruction following: it includes human values, truthfulness, and safety, not just following user prompts.

concepts.name: toxic output generation
concepts.description: The production of harmful, offensive, or abusive text by a language model. Reducing toxic output generation is a common goal in making model behavior safer and more acceptable.

concepts.name: model rankings
concepts.description: Human preference judgments that order multiple model outputs from best to worst. They provide comparative feedback used to train preference-based or reinforcement learning systems.
concepts.match_reason: Model rankings are the preference judgments/feedback signal, while reinforcement learning from human feedback is the training method that uses them.

concepts.name: Atlas
concepts.description: A pre-trained retrieval-augmented language model designed to learn knowledge-intensive tasks from very few training examples. It combines language modeling with document retrieval so it can perform well on tasks that require factual information.
concepts.match_reason: Atlas is a specific retrieval-augmented model, while the candidate is a pre-training approach rather than the same concept.

concepts.name: fact checking
concepts.description: A task that determines whether a claim is supported, refuted, or otherwise verifiable using evidence. It typically requires retrieving and comparing factual information against the claim.

concepts.name: document index
concepts.description: An organized collection of documents or passages used for retrieval in a retrieval-augmented system. The contents of the index can be changed to refresh the evidence available to the model.

concepts.name: MMLU
concepts.description: A benchmark suite of multiple-choice questions spanning many subjects and disciplines. It is used to measure broad language understanding and knowledge across diverse topics.

concepts.name: KILT
concepts.description: A benchmark suite for knowledge-intensive language tasks that evaluates systems on tasks requiring retrieval and grounded generation. It is designed to test whether models can use external evidence effectively.

concepts.name: 统计语言模型
concepts.description: 一种基于词序列统计规律来估计语言出现概率的语言建模方法。它通常利用人工设计的特征或有限上下文来进行下一词预测和语言生成。
concepts.match_reason: “统计语言模型”是语言模型的一种具体类型，候选项是更泛的“language model”，不是同一概念。

concepts.name: 神经语言模型
concepts.description: 一种使用神经网络来学习语言分布的语言模型。它通过从数据中自动学习表示来改进对上下文和语义关系的建模能力。
concepts.match_reason: “神经语言模型”是基于神经网络的语言模型，而候选是传统的“统计语言模型”；二者相关但不是同一概念。

concepts.name: ChatGPT
concepts.description: 一种面向对话的人工智能系统，能够根据用户输入生成自然语言回复。它因强大的交互式语言能力而受到广泛关注。

concepts.name: multi-document question answering
concepts.description: A question answering task in which a system must find and combine relevant evidence scattered across multiple documents. It tests whether the model can retrieve and synthesize information from a set of sources rather than relying on a single passage.
concepts.match_reason: Multi-document question answering is a specific QA setting requiring evidence from multiple documents, while the candidate is the broader question-answering task.

concepts.name: key-value retrieval
concepts.description: A retrieval task in which a model must locate a key in its input and return the associated value. It is often used to measure whether a system can accurately access specific information embedded in a longer context.

concepts.name: long-context language model
concepts.description: A language model designed to process and use very long input sequences. Such models are intended to maintain useful information across extended contexts and support tasks that require accessing distant parts of the input.
concepts.match_reason: A long-context language model is a specific variant designed for very long inputs, while the candidate is the general concept of a language model.

concepts.name: input context
concepts.description: The text provided to a language model as its immediate source of information for generating outputs. It may contain evidence, instructions, or other relevant material that the model is expected to use during prediction.

concepts.name: large language model hallucination
concepts.description: A phenomenon in which a large language model generates content that is fluent and plausible but not grounded in fact. It is a central reliability problem because the output can appear trustworthy while containing false or unsupported information.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a failure mode/phenomenon of large language models, while the candidate is the model itself, not the same concept.

concepts.name: hallucination detection
concepts.description: The task of identifying when a model output contains hallucinated or nonfactual content. Detection methods are designed to flag unreliable generations so they can be corrected, filtered, or further verified.
concepts.match_reason: The candidate is the phenomenon of hallucinated output, while the incoming concept is the task of detecting such output.

concepts.name: hallucination benchmark
concepts.description: An evaluation benchmark designed to measure how often a model produces hallucinated content or how well a system detects it. Benchmarks provide standardized test cases for comparing methods across models and settings.
concepts.match_reason: A hallucination benchmark is an evaluation resource for measuring or comparing hallucination behavior, while hallucination detection is the task of identifying hallucinated content; they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: hallucination mitigation
concepts.description: Methods and strategies intended to reduce the frequency, severity, or impact of hallucinated model outputs. Mitigation can involve training, decoding, retrieval, verification, or system-level controls.
concepts.match_reason: Hallucination mitigation is about reducing or preventing hallucinations, while hallucination detection is only about identifying them; related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: retrieval-augmented large language model
concepts.description: A large language model that incorporates external retrieved information during generation. Retrieval is used to ground responses in relevant evidence and improve factuality, especially in information retrieval settings.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a retrieval-augmented LLM itself, while the closest candidate is a generation method, not the same kind of concept.

concepts.name: large vision-language model hallucination
concepts.description: Hallucination occurring in models that process both visual and textual information, where generated descriptions or answers may conflict with the input image or other visual evidence. It extends the hallucination problem into multimodal systems.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is multimodal hallucination in vision-language models, which is related to but not the same as general large language model hallucination.

concepts.name: knowledge boundary
concepts.description: The limit of what a language model reliably knows or can infer from its parameters and context. Understanding these boundaries helps distinguish likely factual generation from cases where the model may fabricate unsupported content.

concepts.name: query-focused summarization
concepts.description: A summarization task in which the output is tailored to a specific user question rather than to a document’s general content. It aims to synthesize information from a corpus into an answer that addresses the query directly.

concepts.name: entity knowledge graph
concepts.description: A graph structure whose nodes represent entities and whose edges encode relationships among them. It organizes information extracted from source documents so that related entities can be grouped and summarized.

concepts.name: community summary
concepts.description: A summary generated for a group of closely related entities in a graph. It condenses the shared information in that group so it can later support question answering at a higher level of abstraction.

concepts.name: global sensemaking question
concepts.description: A question that asks for an overview, pattern, or theme spanning an entire corpus rather than a single passage. Such questions require synthesizing information across many documents or topics.

concepts.name: Textual subgraph retrieval
concepts.description: The task of retrieving a connected subgraph of text nodes that is relevant to a query or reasoning goal. It aims to select not just individual documents but a coherent graph-structured context that supports multi-hop understanding.

concepts.name: Divide-and-conquer strategy
concepts.description: A problem-solving approach that breaks a task into smaller parts, solves them separately, and combines the results. In retrieval settings, it can be used to efficiently search complex structures by recursively narrowing the candidate space.

concepts.name: Graph context-aware generation
concepts.description: A generation setting in which a model conditions its outputs on graph-structured context as well as textual content. The goal is to let the model use both local text and relational structure when producing responses.
concepts.match_reason: Graph context-aware generation is a broad generation setting, while GraphRAG is a specific retrieval-augmented method for graph-structured documents.

concepts.name: Text view
concepts.description: A representation of graph-structured information that presents the content as plain text. It supports language models by exposing the textual semantics of nodes and edges in a form they can process naturally.

concepts.name: Textual graph
concepts.description: A graph whose nodes, edges, or connected structure are expressed through text. Such graphs combine natural-language content with relational links, enabling both semantic and topological reasoning.
concepts.match_reason: A textual graph is a graph structure expressed through text, while the candidates describe a text-only view, a retrieval task, or the opposite graph-focused view.

concepts.name: Graph reasoning benchmark
concepts.description: An evaluation benchmark designed to measure reasoning over graph-structured information. It tests a model's ability to use relationships, paths, and multi-step connectivity to answer questions or solve tasks.

concepts.name: external knowledge source
concepts.description: A source of information outside a language model that can be retrieved and incorporated into generation. External knowledge sources help ground outputs in up-to-date or domain-specific facts not fully stored in model parameters.

concepts.name: LightRAG
concepts.description: A retrieval-augmented generation framework that incorporates graph structures into text indexing and retrieval. It is designed to improve contextual relevance, retrieval completeness, and efficiency by combining graph-based and vector-based methods.
concepts.match_reason: LightRAG is a distinct graph-plus-vector RAG framework; GraphRAG is a specific graph-based RAG approach, so they are related but not the same concept.

concepts.name: text indexing
concepts.description: The process of organizing text so that relevant information can be efficiently retrieved later. Indexing methods determine how documents or text fragments are represented and searched during retrieval.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the process of indexing text, while the candidate is the indexed collection itself (a document index), so they are not the same concept.

concepts.name: dual-level retrieval system
concepts.description: A retrieval architecture that operates at two levels of abstraction to obtain information. It is designed to support both fine-grained and more global discovery of relevant knowledge.

concepts.name: low-level knowledge discovery
concepts.description: The retrieval of fine-grained, local, or detailed pieces of knowledge from a source. It helps identify specific facts or entities that may be directly relevant to a query.

concepts.name: high-level knowledge discovery
concepts.description: The retrieval of broader, more abstract, or more global knowledge from a source. It helps surface higher-order context and relationships that may not appear in fine-grained retrieval alone.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is specifically high-level knowledge discovery, while the candidates are either the opposite low-level form or a broader two-level system, not the same concept.

concepts.name: related entity
concepts.description: An entity that has a meaningful semantic or relational connection to another entity. Related entities are often retrieved together to preserve context and support more complete answers.

concepts.name: relationship
concepts.description: A semantic connection between entities that expresses how they are associated in a knowledge representation. Relationships help capture structure beyond isolated facts and support relational reasoning.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is the connection itself, while the candidate is an entity that participates in a connection, so they are different concepts.

concepts.name: incremental update algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm that incorporates new data into an existing system without rebuilding everything from scratch. It is used to keep indexed knowledge current while preserving efficiency in changing data environments.

concepts.name: retrieval accuracy
concepts.description: The degree to which a retrieval system returns information that is relevant and correct for a given query. Higher retrieval accuracy means the system is better at finding useful evidence for generation.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a general notion of retrieval quality, while candidate [1] is a specific top-20 evaluation metric, so they are not the same concept.

concepts.name: retrieval efficiency
concepts.description: The degree to which a retrieval system returns relevant information using limited time or computational resources. Higher retrieval efficiency means faster or cheaper access to useful knowledge.
concepts.match_reason: Retrieval efficiency concerns time/resource cost of retrieval, while the candidates describe accuracy or the retrieval procedure, not efficiency.

concepts.name: generative language model
concepts.description: A language model that produces text by predicting and generating sequences of tokens. It can be conditioned on prompts, retrieved evidence, or other context to create fluent natural-language outputs.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a generative language model specifically, while candidate [0] is the broader category of language model, not necessarily the same denotation.

concepts.name: summarization
concepts.description: A text generation task that condenses a longer source into a shorter version while preserving the main points. Retrieval-augmented methods can support summarization by supplying additional context or evidence when needed.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is general summarization, while the candidate is a specific variant: query-focused summarization.

concepts.name: deployment
concepts.description: The process of putting a model or system into practical use in a real-world setting. In language-model applications, deployment raises concerns about performance, safety, and responsible operation.

concepts.name: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation
concepts.description: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation is a retrieval-augmented generation paradigm that uses graph structures to organize knowledge and support retrieval. It aims to improve domain-specific generation by representing relationships explicitly and retrieving context in a way that supports reasoning across linked information.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming term is a general graph-based RAG paradigm, while GraphRAG and LightRAG are specific named frameworks/implementations rather than the same exact concept.

concepts.name: Graph-based retrieval technique
concepts.description: A graph-based retrieval technique is an information retrieval method that operates over graph-structured knowledge rather than flat text. It can traverse connected entities and relations to gather context that preserves relationships and supports multi-step reasoning.
concepts.match_reason: The incoming concept is a broad graph-based retrieval method, while the candidates are specific graph-RAG or subgraph-retrieval variants rather than the same general concept.

concepts.name: Structure-aware knowledge integration algorithm
concepts.description: A structure-aware knowledge integration algorithm is a method for combining retrieved knowledge while preserving the structural information in which it is organized. It uses relationships and graph context to produce more accurate and logically coherent generated outputs.

concepts.name: Context-preserving knowledge retrieval
concepts.description: Context-preserving knowledge retrieval is a retrieval approach that aims to return information together with the surrounding relational context needed to interpret it correctly. By preserving links among entities and concepts, it supports more faithful downstream reasoning and generation.
